prompt: It is 2026. kolm.ai is a SECURITY product for AI-agent vendors. The core insight: a vendor selling an autonomous AI agent into the enterprise already has a SOC 2, but SOC 2 (and security questionnaires, and pentests) were built for human-operated software and say NOTHING about the agent itself: what it can access, what it actually did, whether it can be prompt-injected/hijacked, what data it sends out. So when the buyer's security team vets the agent, the vendor's SOC 2 + questionnaire do not answer the questions, and the enterprise deal stalls for weeks. kolm audits the agent from its logs and produces a SIGNED, offline-verifiable security report the buyer checks themselves against the vendor's public key, mapped to the controls a review cites. We want to LEAD the positioning with the SOC-2-gap: 'SOC 2 doesn't cover your AI agent.' Validate this hard: is leading with the SOC-2 gap the STRONGEST way to sell this (vs leading with 'signed verifiable evidence' or 'agent security')? Write the 6 SHARPEST headline expressions of the SOC-2-gap angle (actual copy lines). Note: we MAP to SOC 2, we are NOT a certification, and 'SOC 2 doesn't cover your agent' is a TRUE gap statement, not a claim to be SOC 2. Flag the risks of this angle (assumes buyer has SOC 2? sounds negative? is SOC 2 the right foil vs ISO 27001 / questionnaires?). Cite real 2026 examples of strong 'X doesn't cover Y' security positioning (@handle/URL + date).
content:
**Yes, leading with the SOC 2 gap is the strongest positioning angle.**[[1]](https://blaxel.ai/blog/soc-2-compliance-ai-guide)[[2]](https://www.penligent.ai/hackinglabs/ai-soc-iso-27001-soc-2-and-the-security-stack-real-ai-teams-need-in-2026/)

Multiple 2025–2026 sources confirm the exact problem: traditional SOC 2 (built on assumptions of static systems, deterministic behavior, and human-mediated access) leaves critical AI-agent risks unaddressed—autonomous decision chains, prompt injection/hijacking, what the agent actually accessed or sent, model drift, unsafe tool use, and behavioral accountability.[[3]](https://soc2auditors.org/insights/soc-2-for-ai-companies/)[[4]](https://agility-at-scale.com/ai/agents/security-and-compliance-for-enterprise-ai-agents/) Enterprise buyers still demand SOC 2 as a de facto contractual requirement for AI/agent vendors, yet it fails to answer security-team questions about the *agent itself*, stalling deals.[[5]](https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/ai-agent-compliance/)

This directly maps to kolm’s value (auditing logs → signed, offline-verifiable report mapped to controls) without claiming to *be* SOC 2 or a certification. It educates on the gap while positioning the product as the precise bridge.

**Alternatives are weaker for lead positioning**:
- “Signed verifiable evidence” describes the *solution* (strong as a follow-on or sub-headline) but lacks the immediate pain-point hook.
- Broad “agent security” or “AI security” is too generic and competes with pentests, red-teaming tools, or general observability—diluting the enterprise-sales specificity.

**Risks of the SOC 2-gap angle** (manageable with framing):
- It assumes the vendor already has (or is pursuing) SOC 2—true for most enterprise-targeted AI/agent companies, but less relevant for early-stage or non-SOC-2 vendors.
- It can sound negative/critical of SOC 2. Mitigate by leading with education (“SOC 2 is table stakes… but…”) rather than attack.
- SOC 2 is the *right primary foil* (most cited de facto requirement in enterprise AI sales), but supplement mentions of ISO 27001, questionnaires, or pentests where relevant; results show SOC 2 dominates the conversation.[[4]](https://agility-at-scale.com/ai/agents/security-and-compliance-for-enterprise-ai-agents/)

**Real 2026 examples of strong “X doesn’t cover Y” security positioning**:
- Multiple Instagram posts (e.g., @various accounts, recurring through April–June 2026): “Your SOC 2 doesn’t cover your AI risk. Full stop. Model drift. Bias testing. Human accountability chains…”[[6]](https://www.instagram.com/p/DUbXr8-j2zu/)[[7]](https://www.instagram.com/p/DW6WSMpDQuF/)
- X post by @Clause5afe (April 9, 2026): “Your SOC 2 doesn’t cover AI risk. No model drift monitoring. No bias testing. No human accountability chain…” (links to their offering).[[8]](https://x.com/Clause5afe/status/2042257228342915441)
- X post by @DarshanSays (May 16, 2026): “Most compliance frameworks were written when ‘system’ meant a server… SOC 2 doesn’t cover autonomous decision chains.”[[9]](https://x.com/DarshanSays/status/2055474843446051223)
- X post by @adefilaadeyinka (April 13, 2026): “SOC 2 doesn’t cover what employees do with AI…” (policy gap).[[10]](https://x.com/adefilaadeyinka/status/2043720792408539435)
- X post by @daniel_roedler (March 20, 2026): “Vendor SOC 2 doesn’t cover your agent’s data flow… Badge ≠ posture.”[[11]](https://x.com/daniel_roedler/status/2034954701666439436)
- Blaxel blog (Feb 18, 2026): “AI agents break traditional SOC 2 assumptions… This creates compliance gaps that existing controls can’t address.”[[1]](https://blaxel.ai/blog/soc-2-compliance-ai-guide)
- LinkedIn/Instagram variants (e.g., Randie Ward and others, 2026): “Their SOC 2 doesn’t cover this—it’s a data processing question, not an infrastructure control question.”[[12]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/first-flight-non-executive-directors_securitytech-ai-saas-activity-7437074509515427840-7gYi)

**6 sharpest SOC 2-gap headline expressions (ready-to-use copy lines)**:
- “SOC 2 Doesn’t Cover Your AI Agent.”
- “Your SOC 2 Report Says Nothing About the Agent.”
- “SOC 2 Is Table Stakes. The Agent Still Needs Its Own Evidence.”
- “Enterprise Deals Stall Here: SOC 2 Misses What the Agent Actually Did.”
- “SOC 2 Maps Your Controls—Not Prompt Injection, Autonomy, or Data Exfil by the Agent.”
- “Buyers Have Your SOC 2. They Still Ask: What Can This Agent Access—and Did It?”

These are concise, benefit-oriented, and directly leverage the validated gap while staying truthful to kolm’s mapping (not certification) approach. Test in outreach or landing pages; the framing converts the stall into an easy “here’s the missing piece” conversation.
